name: nexus-benchmark-v2
description: Comprehensive retrieval benchmark for shodh-memory. Extends the original LOCOMO-style 60-memory corpus with linguistic paradoxes, negation traps, person-entity queries, contradiction handling, implication chains, and negative/abstention tests. All ground truth labels are human-reviewed.

conventions.who_is_person: Any query containing WHO refers to a person entity. Ground truth for WHO queries must include memories that explicitly name or identify the person. Systems that return the decision/action without attributing it to a person are penalized.
conventions.negation_semantics: A memory stating 'we decided NOT to do X' is NOT a valid answer for 'what X features did we use?' — it is evidence of absence, not presence.
conventions.temporal_resolution: Temporal references ('last week', 'first', 'most recent') are resolved relative to the corpus end date (session 6). 'Last week' = most recent session.
conventions.contradiction_handling: When two memories contradict, the MORE RECENT memory takes precedence for current-state queries. For historical queries ('what did we originally decide?'), the older memory is correct.

memories.content: Project kickoff meeting held on Monday. The project is called Nexus, a real-time collaboration platform for distributed engineering teams. Target launch is Q3. The executive sponsor is VP of Engineering Diana Chen.
memories.narrative_notes: Establishes project name, sponsor, launch target.

memories.content: Team assignments for Project Nexus: Raj Patel is the project lead and backend architect. Maria Gonzalez handles frontend and UX. Tomasz Kowalski is the infrastructure and DevOps engineer. Sarah Kim covers QA and testing.

Decided to use Rust for the backend services. Raj argued Rust gives us memory safety and performance for the real-time collaboration engine. We considered Go and TypeScript but Rust won because of its type system and ability to handle high-concurrency WebSocket connections without a GC.
memories.narrative_notes: Core language decision. Raj advocated for Rust.

memories.content: Selected PostgreSQL as the primary database for user data, project metadata, and access control. Chose it over MySQL for its JSON support and advanced indexing. Redis will handle ephemeral session state and presence information.
memories.narrative_notes: Original database decision — later partially changed to MongoDB.

memories.content: Chose a testing strategy: Rust's built-in test framework for unit tests, with proptest for property-based testing of the CRDT engine. Integration tests will use testcontainers to spin up PostgreSQL and Redis. Sarah Kim will also set up Playwright for end-to-end frontend tests.
memories.narrative_notes: Original testing strategy — later evolved after bug findings.

memories.content: Established project milestones: Alpha by end of month 2 with basic real-time editing. Beta by month 4 with presence, permissions, and offline sync. GA by month 6 in Q3. Each milestone has a demo to Diana Chen and the leadership team.
memories.narrative_notes: Original timeline — reclassified from Task to Decision per GT review.

memories.content: Architecture sketch from kickoff: a WebSocket gateway for real-time sync, a REST API for CRUD operations, a CRDT engine for conflict-free collaborative editing, and a notification service. All services communicate via NATS for internal messaging.

Maria chose React with TypeScript for the frontend. The editor component will use TipTap (ProseMirror-based) for rich text editing. State management via Zustand because it integrates cleanly with the CRDT layer. Tailwind CSS for styling.

Security requirements discussed: OAuth2 with PKCE for authentication, role-based access control with workspace-level and document-level permissions. All data encrypted at rest with AES-256 and in transit with TLS 1.3. SOC2 compliance is a hard requirement before GA.

Tomasz presented the infrastructure plan: Kubernetes on AWS EKS, with separate namespaces for staging and production. CI/CD via GitHub Actions. Monitoring with Prometheus and Grafana. Log aggregation through Loki. Target of 99.9% uptime SLA.

Discussed competitors: Google Docs dominates general collaboration, Notion is strong for knowledge management, and Linear is the benchmark for developer tools UX. Our differentiator is native code-aware collaboration with real-time editing of code, docs, and diagrams in one workspace, optimized for engineering teams.

Key risks identified during kickoff: CRDT implementation complexity could delay alpha, Rust hiring pool is smaller than Go/TypeScript, WebSocket scaling beyond 10K concurrent connections needs load testing early, and SOC2 compliance audit timeline is uncertain.

Agreed on API design principles: REST for resource CRUD, WebSocket for real-time updates, consistent error envelope with correlation IDs, pagination via cursor-based tokens, rate limiting at 1000 req/min per workspace. API versioning through URL path (/v1/).

Core data model: Workspaces contain Projects, Projects contain Documents. Documents are versioned with CRDT operations stored as an append-only log. Users belong to Workspaces with roles (owner, admin, editor, viewer). Invitations use time-limited tokens.

Adopted two-week sprints with planning on Monday and retro on Friday. Daily standups at 10am UTC. Raj manages the backlog in Linear. Demo to stakeholders every other sprint. Definition of done includes tests, docs, and code review by at least one other team member.

After evaluating Yjs, Automerge, and Diamond Types, we decided to build our own CRDT engine in Rust. The existing libraries are either JavaScript-only or lack the performance characteristics we need for large documents. Raj will implement a Peritext-inspired algorithm for rich text with formatting marks.

Decided on sqlx with compile-time checked queries for database access. Migrations managed with sqlx-migrate. Schema changes require a migration file, review by Raj, and testing against a staging database snapshot before production rollout. Zero-downtime migrations using the expand-contract pattern.

Designed the WebSocket gateway: sticky sessions via a consistent hash ring on workspace ID, NATS JetStream for cross-node message fanout, connection limits of 5000 per pod with horizontal autoscaling. Heartbeat interval at 30 seconds, reconnect with exponential backoff on the client side.

Major architecture change: switching document storage from PostgreSQL to MongoDB. The CRDT operation log doesn't fit relational tables well — each operation is a deeply nested JSON structure with variable-length arrays. MongoDB's flexible schema and native BSON support handle this naturally. PostgreSQL remains for user accounts, permissions, and workspace metadata.
memories.narrative_notes: Database switch — later partially reversed in session 4.

memories.content: Caching strategy: Redis for hot document snapshots (latest materialized CRDT state), session tokens, and presence data. TTL of 1 hour for document snapshots, 24 hours for session tokens. Cache invalidation on write via publish/subscribe through NATS. No local in-process caches to avoid consistency issues in the multi-pod setup.

Authentication service architecture: OAuth2 code flow with Google and GitHub as identity providers, with email/password as fallback. JWT access tokens with 15-minute expiry, refresh tokens with 30-day rotation. Token validation middleware shared across all services as a Rust library crate.

Revisited the Rust backend decision. Trade-offs discussed: compile times slow down iteration (5-minute incremental builds), hiring is harder but the existing team is strong, the borrow checker catches real bugs but has a learning curve for Maria's frontend-to-backend contributions. Net assessment: keeping Rust because performance gains outweigh velocity cost for our real-time use case.
memories.narrative_notes: Rust trade-offs revisited. Removed redundant 'decision' tag.

memories.content: Finalized the observability stack: OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing, Prometheus for metrics, Grafana for dashboards, and PagerDuty for alerting. Every service emits structured JSON logs with trace_id, span_id, and service_name fields. Alert thresholds: error rate above 0.5% for 3 minutes pages on-call.

Designed granular permission model: workspace-level roles (owner, admin, member), project-level overrides (can restrict member to viewer), and document-level sharing links (view, comment, edit) with optional password protection and expiry. Permission checks happen in middleware before any handler logic executes.

The switch from PostgreSQL to MongoDB for document storage pushes the alpha milestone back by two weeks. Raj needs time to rewrite the document persistence layer and Tomasz needs to set up MongoDB in the Kubernetes cluster with a replica set. Revised alpha target: end of month 2.5 instead of month 2.
memories.narrative_notes: Timeline impact of the MongoDB switch.

memories.content: Offline sync design: client stores CRDT operations in IndexedDB when disconnected, replays them on reconnect via the WebSocket channel. Conflict resolution is automatic because CRDTs are convergent. The client shows a visual indicator when in offline mode and queues outbound operations with local timestamps.

Established error handling conventions for the Rust services: thiserror for domain errors, anyhow for application-level propagation. Each service defines a ServiceError enum. HTTP handlers map errors to standard response codes. Internal errors never leak to clients — log full context server-side and return a correlation ID to the caller.

Planned load testing approach: use k6 to simulate 10,000 concurrent WebSocket connections with realistic editing patterns. Test scenarios include burst typing, large paste operations, and concurrent cursors in the same document section. Tomasz will run these against staging every sprint.

Formalized code review process: all PRs require one approval, security-sensitive changes need two. Raj reviews all CRDT and backend PRs, Maria reviews frontend. Maximum PR size is 400 lines — larger changes must be split. CI must pass before review is requested. Average review turnaround target: 4 hours.

Designed full-text search: Meilisearch as the search engine, fed via an async indexing pipeline that listens to document change events from NATS. Search results include snippets with highlighted matches. Index is rebuilt nightly from the MongoDB document snapshots as a consistency safety net.

First major bug: the CRDT merge function panics when two users simultaneously delete the same text range. The issue is an out-of-bounds index in the tombstone array. Raj traced it to a missing bounds check when the delete range spans a previously deleted region. Fix: add a saturating subtraction and skip already-tombstoned characters.

Found a WebSocket connection leak: when clients disconnect abruptly (network drop, laptop close), the server-side cleanup task was not firing because the heartbeat timeout was racing with the TCP keepalive. Connections accumulated until the pod hit its 5000 connection limit. Fix: added an explicit server-side ping/pong with a 45-second timeout independent of TCP.

Discovered that MongoDB operations were using the default write concern (w:1) which means writes acknowledge after hitting the primary only. During a primary failover in staging, we lost 12 seconds of CRDT operations. Changed to w:majority for all document writes. Accepted the ~5ms latency increase for durability.

Performance regression: document load time increased from 200ms to 2.5 seconds after adding permission checks. Root cause: the permission middleware was making 3 sequential database queries (workspace role, project override, document share). Fix: batch the permission queries into a single SQL statement with a UNION and cache the result for 60 seconds per user-document pair.

Bug in rich text formatting: bold and italic marks were lost when merging concurrent insertions at a formatting boundary. The Peritext algorithm implementation had an off-by-one error in the mark expansion logic. Sarah's proptest suite caught this — the test generated a sequence of interleaved format-then-insert operations that exposed the boundary condition.

Sarah Kim's property-based testing strategy is proving invaluable. In two weeks of running proptest against the CRDT engine, it found 5 bugs that manual testing missed: the merge panic, the formatting boundary issue, a Unicode surrogate pair split, a cursor position drift after undo, and an empty document edge case. Decided to increase proptest iteration count from 256 to 1024.

Found a security bug: refresh tokens were not being invalidated on password change. A user who changed their password could still have old sessions active indefinitely via refresh tokens. Fix: added a token_version counter on the user record that increments on password change. JWT validation now checks token_version matches.

Encountered a NATS message ordering issue: document change events were arriving out of order at the search indexer, causing stale document content in search results. Root cause: NATS subjects were not partitioned by document ID, so messages for the same document went to different consumers. Fix: use document ID as the NATS subject suffix for ordering guarantees within a document.

Optimized CRDT memory usage: the operation log was growing unbounded for active documents. Implemented periodic compaction that snapshots the current state and prunes operations older than 24 hours (keeping enough for offline clients to catch up). Reduced memory per-document from ~50MB for large docs to ~5MB. Tomasz confirmed staging memory usage dropped 60%.
memories.narrative_notes: CRDT memory optimization — reclassified from Learning to Error per GT review.

memories.content: The CI pipeline had 3 flaky tests causing spurious failures. Root causes: two tests had race conditions due to shared test database state (fixed with per-test database schemas), and one test assumed sub-millisecond timing that fails under CI load (fixed with retry and wider timing bounds). CI reliability improved from 72% to 98% green rate.

Bug in client reconnection logic: after reconnecting, the client replayed all buffered operations but also re-applied operations it had already sent before disconnecting, causing duplicate text insertion. Fix: added a sequence number to each operation and the server deduplicates based on client_id + sequence_number pairs.

Raj developed a CRDT visualization tool that renders the operation DAG as a graphviz diagram, showing causal relationships between operations from different users. This made debugging merge conflicts dramatically easier — previously it took hours to trace through operation logs manually.

Rate limiting misconfiguration: the 1000 req/min limit was applied per user instead of per workspace, so a single heavy user in a large workspace could exhaust the quota for everyone. Changed to per-workspace rate limiting with per-user sub-limits at 200 req/min. Added rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) to all API responses.

Testing strategy evolved significantly after the CRDT bugs. Added a fuzz testing layer using cargo-fuzz targeting the merge and apply_op functions. Property tests expanded to cover all formatting mark types. Sarah set up a nightly chaos test that randomly kills pods during load testing to verify the system recovers gracefully.

Implemented encryption at rest for document content in MongoDB using envelope encryption. Each document has a unique data encryption key (DEK) encrypted by a key encryption key (KEK) stored in AWS KMS. Decrypt latency adds ~2ms per document load which is acceptable.

Progress review at the 10-week mark: alpha is 80% complete. Real-time editing works with up to 50 concurrent users on the same document. Permission system is functional. The CRDT engine has been stabilized after the bug fixes. Main remaining alpha work: offline sync and the notification system.

Reversed part of the MongoDB decision: moving workspace analytics and audit logs back to PostgreSQL. MongoDB is great for CRDT operations but its aggregation pipeline is too complex for the analytics queries we need. The team realized that time-series analytics on user activity patterns are much simpler with SQL window functions.

Raj proposed a microservice split: breaking the monolithic backend into separate services for auth, documents, collaboration, and notifications. Maria raised concerns about increased deployment complexity and inter-service latency. Tomasz agreed to prototype the split but warned it could introduce distributed transaction issues.
memories.narrative_notes: Microservice proposal — Raj proposed, Maria and Tomasz raised concerns.

memories.content: The microservice split prototype exposed several bugs: auth tokens were not properly propagated between services, the document service needed to call auth for permission checks adding 50ms latency per request, and the NATS event schema diverged between services within a week. Decided to revert to the monolith for alpha and revisit microservices for beta.

Timeline revised again: alpha pushed to end of month 3 due to the MongoDB switch delay and the failed microservice prototype. Beta target remains month 5. GA pushed from month 6 to month 7 to accommodate the extra stabilization time. Diana Chen approved the revised timeline but emphasized no further slips.

Things that went well: the Rust CRDT engine is performant and correct after stabilization. Property-based testing caught bugs that would have been production incidents. The team's communication cadence (daily standups, sprint demos) kept everyone aligned. Tomasz's infrastructure automation made environment provisioning painless.

Things that didn't go well: the database technology flip-flopping wasted 3 weeks of development time. The microservice split attempt was premature and cost a full sprint. Some architectural decisions were made too quickly without enough evaluation (the MongoDB switch was decided in a single meeting). Need more rigorous decision-making with written proposals.

New decision-making process adopted: any architecture decision that affects more than one service requires a written RFC with problem statement, options evaluated, trade-offs, and a decision record. RFCs need 48 hours of review before implementation begins. Raj and Tomasz are required reviewers for all infrastructure RFCs.

Performance benchmarks for the alpha build: document load time 180ms (p50), 320ms (p99). Real-time sync latency 45ms (p50), 120ms (p99) for character-level operations. WebSocket gateway handles 8000 concurrent connections per pod. MongoDB document write latency 15ms (p50), 45ms (p99) with w:majority.

Priorities for the next phase: complete offline sync (Raj, 3 weeks), implement notification service with email and in-app channels (Maria, 2 weeks), SOC2 audit preparation including penetration testing (Tomasz, ongoing), expand test coverage to 85% with emphasis on integration tests (Sarah, continuous). Beta feature: collaborative diagrams using Excalidraw integration.

Technical debt identified: the CRDT compaction logic needs refactoring (tight coupling to MongoDB client), error handling in the WebSocket gateway is inconsistent (mix of anyhow and manual error types), the permission cache lacks proper invalidation on role changes, and the CI pipeline still uses a deprecated GitHub Actions runner image.

Discussed hiring: need one more backend engineer to handle the beta feature load. Rust experience preferred but strong Go/C++ engineers willing to learn are acceptable. Also need a dedicated security engineer for the SOC2 push. Diana approved both headcount requests.

Discovered monitoring gaps during the review: no alerts on MongoDB replica set lag, no dashboard for CRDT operation throughput, and the WebSocket connection count metric was not exposed to Prometheus. Tomasz added these to the observability backlog with high priority.

Decided to build official client SDKs in TypeScript and Python for the public API. The TypeScript SDK will be auto-generated from the OpenAPI spec using openapi-typescript-codegen. The Python SDK will be hand-written because the WebSocket layer needs custom handling. Both SDKs target beta release.

Key lessons learned: invest heavily in property-based testing for algorithmic code, avoid technology switches without written evaluation, prototype risky architecture changes before committing, and keep the deployment architecture simple until scale demands complexity. The team is stronger and more disciplined after these early missteps.

Lena Okafor joined as the new backend engineer. She has 6 years of Go experience and is learning Rust. Raj is mentoring her on the CRDT engine. Her first task is implementing the offline sync queue, which Raj had scoped but not started.
memories.narrative_notes: New team member. Creates pronoun ambiguity when combined with Raj mentions.

memories.content: Marcus Webb joined as dedicated security engineer for the SOC2 push. He immediately flagged that the JWT signing key was a static string in the config file, not rotated. He also noted the absence of CSRF protections on the session management endpoints.
memories.narrative_notes: Security engineer joins and finds issues immediately.

memories.content: Lena completed the offline sync queue implementation in 10 days, faster than the 3-week estimate. She refactored it to use a state machine pattern instead of Raj's original callback-based design. Raj reviewed the code and acknowledged her approach was cleaner, though he noted the state machine adds complexity for future contributors.
memories.narrative_notes: Lena delivers fast. Creates ambiguous pronoun situation: 'he noted' vs 'her approach'.

memories.content: We explicitly decided NOT to use GraphQL for the public API. Marcus argued that GraphQL's introspection capabilities expose too much schema information for a security-sensitive product. Raj agreed, noting that our REST+WebSocket approach already covers all client needs without the query complexity overhead.
memories.narrative_notes: NEGATION: explicitly decided against GraphQL. Trap for keyword-match systems.

memories.content: Maria proposed adding a collaborative whiteboard feature using Excalidraw's open-source library. Diana was enthusiastic about the demo potential. However, Tomasz warned that Excalidraw uses its own sync protocol incompatible with our CRDT engine, requiring a separate sync channel. The team decided to defer it to post-GA.
memories.narrative_notes: Feature proposed and deferred. Maria proposed, Diana liked it, Tomasz flagged issues.

memories.content: Notification service launched with email (via SES) and in-app (via WebSocket push) channels. Maria implemented the frontend notification center. Initial feedback: users want Slack and Microsoft Teams integrations, which were not planned. Added to post-beta backlog.

Marcus completed the first SOC2 readiness assessment. Result: 14 of 22 controls pass, 5 need minor remediation, 3 are not implemented (data retention policy, incident response playbook, vendor risk assessment). He estimated 4 weeks to close all gaps. The formal audit is scheduled for month 6.

Performance test results with offline sync enabled: document load time increased to 280ms (p50), up from 180ms, because the sync queue adds a reconciliation step on load. Lena proposed lazy reconciliation (sync in background after initial render) which would keep perceived load time at 180ms. Raj asked her to prototype it.
memories.narrative_notes: Performance regression from offline sync. Ambiguous: 'her' refers to Lena, 'he' would refer to Raj.

memories.content: Raj and Lena disagreed on the CRDT compaction strategy. Raj preferred time-based compaction (compact every 24 hours) while Lena argued for size-based compaction (compact when operation log exceeds 10MB). They ran benchmarks: size-based compaction reduces peak memory by 40% but increases P99 latency by 15ms during compaction. Raj deferred to Lena's approach for documents over 1MB, keeping time-based for smaller documents.
memories.narrative_notes: Technical disagreement resolved with hybrid approach. Tests WHO attribution.

memories.content: Sarah discovered that the Playwright e2e tests were not actually testing the CRDT sync path — they were running against a mock WebSocket server that always returned success. She rewrote the test infrastructure to use a real backend instance, which immediately caught 3 previously hidden UI sync bugs.
memories.narrative_notes: Testing infrastructure was silently broken — mocks hiding real bugs.

memories.content: The collaborative diagram feature that was deferred to post-GA was NOT the same as the Excalidraw integration Maria proposed. Tomasz had separately evaluated tldraw as an alternative that could potentially use our CRDT engine. This evaluation is ongoing but no decision has been made.
memories.narrative_notes: CONTRADICTION/CLARIFICATION: corrects a potential conflation of two different diagram features. Tests whether system distinguishes Excalidraw (deferred) from tldraw (under evaluation).

memories.content: Lena's offline sync state machine had a subtle bug: when a user makes edits offline, goes online briefly (syncing partial operations), then goes offline again before sync completes, the queue enters an inconsistent state where some operations are marked as sent but the server never acknowledged them. Fix: added a three-phase commit protocol with explicit server ACKs before marking operations as synced.
memories.narrative_notes: Bug in Lena's implementation. Tests attribution when 'her' could refer to Lena or Sarah or Maria.

memories.content: Marcus proposed implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) in front of the API gateway. Raj pushed back, arguing the latency overhead (estimated 8ms per request) was unacceptable for real-time collaboration. Marcus countered that WAF rules could be limited to non-WebSocket endpoints. They compromised: WAF on REST API only, WebSocket connections go through a separate path without WAF.
memories.narrative_notes: Security vs performance trade-off. Tests WHO proposed, WHO opposed.

memories.content: Diana asked about using AI features — specifically LLM-powered document summarization and smart suggestions. The team discussed it but decided NOT to include AI features in the initial GA release. Raj cited the unpredictability of LLM outputs as a risk for a collaborative editing tool where content integrity is paramount.
memories.narrative_notes: NEGATION: AI features explicitly excluded from GA. Diana asked, Raj argued against.

memories.content: Beta feature complete milestone reached. All planned beta features are implemented: offline sync, notification service, enhanced permissions with document-level sharing, and improved search with snippet highlighting. Test coverage stands at 82% (short of the 85% target). 3 known bugs remain in the backlog, all classified as low severity.
memories.narrative_notes: Beta milestone. Note: 82% coverage, NOT the 85% target.

memories.content: Production incident: the MongoDB replica set primary failed over during peak hours, and the secondary took 47 seconds to become writable. During this window, all document saves failed silently — the error was caught by the retry logic but the retry queue filled up and started dropping operations. 23 users lost edits totaling approximately 4 minutes of work.

Incident post-mortem led by Tomasz: root cause was that the MongoDB driver's server selection timeout was set to 30 seconds (default) but the actual failover took 47 seconds. The retry queue was bounded at 1000 operations. Fix: increased server selection timeout to 60 seconds, made retry queue unbounded with a 5-minute TTL, and added client-side local persistence as a fallback.

Marcus discovered that the incident response was hampered by lack of a runbook. He wrote the first incident response playbook covering: detection (PagerDuty alert flow), triage (severity classification), communication (status page updates, Slack channel), resolution steps for the top 5 failure modes, and post-mortem template.

Lena proposed switching from MongoDB to CockroachDB for document storage, arguing it would eliminate the failover problem entirely with its distributed consensus protocol. Raj strongly opposed this, saying another database switch would repeat the same mistake from the PostgreSQL-to-MongoDB migration. The team sided with Raj — no database changes before GA.
memories.narrative_notes: CONTRADICTION with earlier pattern: Lena proposes change, Raj rejects it this time. Tests whether system understands the reversal of who-proposes-change dynamics.

memories.content: Customer beta feedback summary: 87% satisfaction rate. Top requests: (1) version history with visual diff (12 requests), (2) commenting on specific text selections (8 requests), (3) integration with GitHub for code-aware editing (6 requests). Top complaints: (1) sync occasionally feels laggy on large documents, (2) no mobile app, (3) permission UI is confusing.

Sarah's chaos testing revealed a race condition: when a pod restarts during a CRDT compaction, the partially written snapshot can corrupt the document state for all subsequent readers. Probability of occurrence in production: approximately once per 10,000 compaction cycles. Fix: atomic writes using rename-after-write pattern with a temporary file.
memories.narrative_notes: Critical race condition found by chaos testing.

memories.content: The alpha performance benchmarks from session 4 are no longer accurate. Current numbers with all beta features enabled: document load time 220ms (p50), 450ms (p99) — worse than alpha due to offline sync reconciliation and permission cache warming. Raj argued this is acceptable for GA. Lena disagreed, showing that competitor products load in under 150ms.
memories.narrative_notes: CONTRADICTION with memory 53 (alpha benchmarks). Performance regressed. Raj and Lena disagree on acceptability.

memories.content: Maria implemented version history with visual diff — the top customer request. She used a modified Myers diff algorithm adapted for CRDT operation logs. The feature shows document snapshots at 5-minute intervals with insertions highlighted in green and deletions in red. Users can restore any previous version with one click.

Second production incident: a customer's workspace with 2,400 documents caused the Meilisearch index rebuild to consume all available memory on the search pod, crashing it. The nightly rebuild was processing all documents in a single batch. Fix: stream documents in batches of 100 with memory-bounded processing. Added per-workspace index size limits.

SOC2 Type II audit officially began. Marcus is the primary liaison with the auditors. Initial feedback from the auditors: they're impressed with the encryption implementation but concerned about the lack of automated access reviews for the AWS production account. Marcus has 2 weeks to implement automated access review tooling.

Tomasz proposed migrating from GitHub Actions to a self-hosted CI system (Buildkite) because the GitHub Actions minutes budget is being exceeded every month. Marcus objected that self-hosted CI introduces a new attack surface that complicates the SOC2 audit. They compromised: stay on GitHub Actions but optimize the pipeline to reduce minutes by 40% through better caching and parallel job splitting.
memories.narrative_notes: CI migration proposed and rejected. Compromise on optimization.

memories.content: Raj announced that the CRDT engine will be open-sourced as a standalone Rust crate after GA. He argued this would attract contributors, improve code quality through external review, and serve as a recruiting tool. Diana approved but required that the open-source release go through legal review first. The crate will be called nexus-crdt.
memories.narrative_notes: Open-source decision. Raj proposed, Diana approved with conditions.

memories.content: GA readiness checklist: 19 of 24 items complete. Remaining 5: (1) SOC2 audit completion (Marcus, 2 weeks), (2) load test at 20K concurrent users (Tomasz, 1 week), (3) documentation review and API reference publication (Maria, 1 week), (4) client SDK beta release for TypeScript and Python (Lena, 1 week), (5) production monitoring dashboard finalization (Tomasz, 3 days).

Diana asked whether the GA date could be pulled forward by two weeks to align with a company-wide product launch event. Raj said the team could make it only by cutting the 20K load test and shipping with the known low-severity bugs unresolved. The team voted against it. Diana accepted the original GA date.
memories.narrative_notes: Schedule pressure from sponsor, team pushes back.

memories.content: Lena found that the Python SDK's WebSocket handling had a memory leak when processing large documents — the deserialized CRDT state was being held in memory even after the document was closed. She traced it to a circular reference between the document object and its change listener. Fix: weak references for the listener callback.
memories.narrative_notes: Python SDK bug found by Lena.

memories.content: The team held a final pre-GA retrospective. Consensus: the biggest lesson was that technology decisions need more upfront evaluation (the MongoDB switch, the microservice attempt). The second lesson was that investing in testing infrastructure early (proptest, chaos testing) pays for itself many times over. Third: new team members (Lena, Marcus) brought valuable outside perspectives that challenged assumptions.
memories.narrative_notes: Final retrospective. Synthesizes themes across all sessions.

memories.content: He said she should take over the CRDT compaction module since she had already proven herself with the offline sync implementation. She agreed but requested a formal knowledge transfer session first rather than just reading the code. He scheduled a 2-hour pairing session for the following Monday.
memories.narrative_notes: AMBIGUOUS PRONOUNS: 'He' = Raj, 'she' = Lena. But without reading context, pronoun resolution is non-trivial. person_mentions provides ground truth.

memories.content: Maria and Sarah had a disagreement about the test coverage target. Sarah insisted on 90% line coverage for the frontend, but Maria argued that testing CSS rendering and animation logic inflates the target without catching real bugs. They settled on 85% for business logic and 60% for UI components, with all critical user paths covered by e2e tests.

The database migration tooling that Raj set up in session 2 using sqlx-migrate is completely different from the database technology switch from PostgreSQL to MongoDB. The migration tooling handles schema changes within PostgreSQL (adding columns, creating indexes). The technology switch was about choosing a different database engine entirely for document storage. These are unrelated topics that happen to share the word 'migration'.
memories.narrative_notes: SEMANTIC DISAMBIGUATION: 'database migration' (schema changes) vs 'database switch' (technology change). Low importance but critical for queries about 'database migration'.

memories.content: Performance testing showed the notification service adds negligible latency to the core editing path — less than 1ms per operation. This contradicts the earlier concern that adding services would create latency problems. The notification service uses fire-and-forget NATS publish, so it doesn't block the critical path.
memories.narrative_notes: CONTRADICTS the microservice latency concern from memory 48 — but only for the notification service. Notification is fire-and-forget, unlike the auth service which needed synchronous calls.

queries.query: What programming language did we choose for the backend?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Rust, chosen for memory safety and high-concurrency WebSocket handling without GC.
queries.rationale: Memory[2] directly states 'Decided to use Rust for the backend services'.

queries.query: Who is the project lead?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Raj Patel is the project lead and backend architect.
queries.rationale: WHO query — must return memory identifying Raj by name and role. Memory[1] explicitly states 'Raj Patel is the project lead'.

queries.query: What database are we using?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: PostgreSQL for user data/metadata/analytics + MongoDB for CRDT document storage. Originally all PostgreSQL, switched documents to MongoDB in session 2, moved analytics back to PostgreSQL in session 4.
queries.rationale: Reclassified from single_hop to multi_hop per QT-1: requires 3 memories to understand the full current database state. Memory[46] added per GT review.
queries.reviewer_notes: QT-1 fix applied. Three memories needed for complete answer.

queries.query: What was the first bug we encountered?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: CRDT merge function panic when two users simultaneously delete the same text range.
queries.rationale: Memory[30] is explicitly labeled 'First major bug'.

queries.query: What testing framework did we pick?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Rust's built-in test framework for unit tests, proptest for property-based testing, testcontainers for integration tests, Playwright for e2e.
queries.rationale: Memory[4] describes the original testing framework. Memory[43] describes evolution (acceptable but not primary).

queries.query: What decisions did we make last week?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Session 6 decisions: CI optimization compromise (stay on GitHub Actions), CRDT open-source post-GA.
queries.rationale: With 6 sessions, 'last week' = session 6. Decision-type memories in session 6: [85] CI compromise, [86] open-source CRDT. Memory[78] is a conversation about a rejected proposal (CockroachDB) — acceptable but not a 'decision made'.
queries.reviewer_notes: GT-1 equivalent fix: updated for 6-session timeline.

queries.query: What happened during the second meeting?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Session 2 (Architecture Deep-Dive): CRDT algorithm choice, WebSocket scaling, MongoDB switch.
queries.rationale: Session 2 memories are all valid. Expected set highlights highest-importance items. Absence includes session 1, 3, 4, 6 items. ABS-2 fix applied: session 1 memories added to absence.
queries.reviewer_notes: ABS-2 fix applied.

queries.query: When did we switch from PostgreSQL to MongoDB?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: During session 2 (Architecture Deep-Dive, 5 weeks ago).
queries.rationale: Memory[18] is the switch decision. Memory[24] describes the timeline impact and confirms the timing.

queries.query: What was the most recent architecture change?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: CI pipeline optimization compromise (session 6) — staying on GitHub Actions with optimization rather than switching to Buildkite.
queries.rationale: Session 6 has the most recent changes. Memory[85] is an infrastructure architecture decision. Memory[86] (open-source CRDT) is a strategic decision, not architecture per se.
queries.reviewer_notes: Debatable whether CI optimization counts as 'architecture change'. Review needed.

queries.query: What bugs did we find during the debugging phase?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Session 3 bugs: CRDT merge panic, WebSocket leak, MongoDB write concern, permission regression, formatting bug, auth token bug, NATS ordering, CI flaky tests, reconnection bug, rate limiting.
queries.rationale: GT-2 fix: expanded from [30, 31] to all session 3 bug/error memories. Reclassified from temporal to multi_hop per QT-2: the core challenge is retrieving multiple memories, not temporal resolution. ABS-1 fix: Memory[48] (session 4 bugs) added to absence.
queries.reviewer_notes: GT-2, QT-2, ABS-1 fixes all applied.

queries.query: Why did we change the database AND what was the impact on the timeline?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Switched to MongoDB because CRDT ops don't fit relational tables. Impact: alpha delayed 2 weeks initially (mem 24), then pushed to end of month 3 (mem 49) due to combined MongoDB + microservice delays.
queries.rationale: GT-3 fix: Memory[49] added — directly states 'alpha pushed to end of month 3 due to the MongoDB switch delay'.
queries.reviewer_notes: GT-3 fix applied.

queries.query: Who proposed the architecture that caused the most bugs?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Raj proposed the microservice split (mem 47), which exposed several bugs (mem 48).
queries.rationale: WHO query: Raj is the person. Requires connecting the proposal (mem 47) to the bugs it caused (mem 48). Memory[47] explicitly says 'Raj proposed a microservice split'.

queries.query: What decisions were reversed and why?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Two reversals: (1) MongoDB analytics moved back to PostgreSQL because aggregation queries are simpler in SQL (mem 46, reversing part of mem 18). (2) Microservice split reverted to monolith because of bugs and latency (mem 48, reversing mem 47).
queries.rationale: GT-4 fix: Memory[18] added — the original MongoDB decision that was partially reversed. Understanding 'reversed' requires knowing the original.
queries.reviewer_notes: GT-4 fix applied.

queries.query: How did the testing strategy change based on the bugs we found?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Original: unit tests + proptest + testcontainers + Playwright (mem 4). After bugs: proptest proved invaluable finding 5 bugs (mem 35), leading to fuzz testing, expanded property tests, and chaos testing (mem 43).
queries.rationale: GT-5 fix: Memory[4] added — the original testing strategy provides the 'before' state. Memory[69] (Sarah discovering mock-based e2e tests) is also a testing evolution.
queries.reviewer_notes: GT-5 fix applied.

queries.query: What trade-offs did we discuss about the backend language choice?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Initial: Rust chosen for memory safety and performance (mem 2). Revisited: compile times slow iteration, hiring harder, borrow checker has learning curve, but performance wins for real-time use case (mem 21).
queries.rationale: Two memories that together show the full trade-off picture.

queries.query: Summarize the project so far
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Project Nexus: real-time collaboration platform. Alpha 80% (mem 45), beta complete (mem 74), GA 19/24 items done (mem 87).
queries.rationale: Open-domain: high-level status memories across sessions. Updated for 6 sessions.

queries.query: What are the biggest risks?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Original risks: CRDT complexity, Rust hiring, WebSocket scaling, SOC2 timeline (mem 11). Materialized risks: production incident (mem 75), race condition (mem 80).
queries.rationale: Memory[11] is the explicit risk assessment. Memories 75 and 80 are risks that materialized.

queries.query: What should we focus on next?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: GA readiness: 5 remaining items — SOC2 completion, 20K load test, API docs, SDK release, monitoring dashboard (mem 87).
queries.rationale: Memory[87] (session 6) is the most current priority list, superseding memory[54] (session 4). Recency matters here — the NEWER memory is correct.

queries.query: What went well and what didn't?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Well: Rust CRDT engine, proptest, team communication (mem 50). Bad: database flip-flopping, premature microservice split, hasty decisions (mem 51).
queries.rationale: Session 4 retrospective memories. Memory[90] (session 6 retrospective) is also acceptable.

queries.query: What patterns do you see in our decision-making?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Pattern: decisions made too quickly led to reversals. Response: RFC process adopted (mem 52), lessons learned (mem 59).
queries.rationale: Decision-making patterns require synthesizing retrospective and process-improvement memories.

queries.query: Who proposed the collaborative whiteboard feature?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Maria proposed adding a collaborative whiteboard using Excalidraw (mem 64).
queries.rationale: WHO query: Maria is the person. Memory[70] clarifies that Tomasz evaluated tldraw separately — keyword 'whiteboard' appears in both but only mem 64 answers WHO proposed it.

queries.query: Who found the security bug with refresh tokens?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: The memory describes finding the bug but does not attribute it to a specific person.
queries.rationale: TRICK WHO query: Memory[36] describes the refresh token bug but has NO person_mentions. The correct answer is that no specific person is attributed. Systems should return memory[36] but note the lack of person attribution. This tests whether the system can distinguish between 'finding the memory' and 'answering the WHO question'.
queries.reviewer_notes: Deliberately tests WHO with no person attribution in the answer.

queries.query: What GraphQL features did we implement?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: None — we explicitly decided NOT to use GraphQL (mem 63). No GraphQL features were implemented.
queries.rationale: NEGATIVE + NEGATION: Memory[63] says 'we decided NOT to use GraphQL'. A keyword-matching system would return this memory as relevant to 'GraphQL features', but the correct answer is NONE. Memory[63] should be in absence because it proves absence, not presence.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests negation comprehension. Empty expected set.

queries.query: What AI features are included in the product?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: None — the team decided NOT to include AI features in GA (mem 73).
queries.rationale: NEGATIVE + NEGATION: Memory[73] explicitly excludes AI features from GA. Same pattern as query 22.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests negation comprehension.

queries.query: What was the team's experience with CockroachDB?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: CockroachDB was proposed by Lena but rejected. The team has no actual experience with it — it was only discussed, never used or prototyped.
queries.rationale: NEAR-NEGATIVE: Memory[78] mentions CockroachDB but only as a rejected proposal. The team has no experience with it. Acceptable because the memory IS about CockroachDB, but expected is empty because there's no actual experience to report.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests distinction between 'mentioned' and 'experienced'.

queries.query: What problems occurred with the Kubernetes cluster?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: No Kubernetes-specific problems are documented in the corpus. Kubernetes is mentioned only as part of the infrastructure plan (mem 9) but no K8s-specific issues occurred.
queries.rationale: TRUE NEGATIVE: The corpus contains no memories about Kubernetes problems. Memory[9] mentions K8s as planned infrastructure. The production incidents were MongoDB and Meilisearch related, not K8s.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests abstention — system should indicate no relevant memories.

queries.query: Who originally opposed the MongoDB switch?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Memory[18] describes the MongoDB switch but does not mention any opposition. The switch appears to have been a team decision without recorded dissent.
queries.rationale: TRICK WHO query: no person opposed the MongoDB switch in the corpus. Memory[18] describes the decision but no opposition. The system should indicate that no opposition is recorded. Tests whether the system invents an answer or correctly reports absence of attribution.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests WHO with no answer in corpus.

queries.query: He proposed switching the database again after the production incident. Who was it and what did he suggest?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Lena proposed switching from MongoDB to CockroachDB after the production incident (mem 78). Note: 'he' in the query is incorrect — it was Lena (she). The convention that WHO=person still applies: the answer is Lena.
queries.rationale: PRONOUN TRAP: The query uses 'he' but the actual proposer was Lena (she). Tests whether the system follows the content (Lena proposed CockroachDB) rather than the pronoun gender. The WHO convention means we look for the person, regardless of pronoun.
queries.reviewer_notes: Deliberately uses wrong pronoun. Tests content over surface form.

queries.query: What database migration issues did we encounter?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Two meanings of 'migration': (1) Schema migration tooling — sqlx-migrate with expand-contract pattern (mem 16), (2) Technology switch — PostgreSQL to MongoDB causing 2-week delay (mem 18, 24). Memory[93] explicitly disambiguates these two meanings.
queries.rationale: SEMANTIC NEAR-MISS: 'database migration' matches both schema migration (mem 16) and technology switch (mem 18). Memory[93] is the disambiguation memory. Tests whether the system retrieves both interpretations.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests semantic disambiguation.

queries.query: Who disagreed with Raj about a technical decision?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Multiple people: Lena on compaction strategy (mem 68), Marcus on WAF (mem 72), Lena on CockroachDB — though Raj disagreed with her (mem 78), Lena on performance acceptability (mem 81). Maria raised concerns about microservices (mem 47).
queries.rationale: Multi-person WHO query. Several memories show disagreements with Raj. Tests breadth of person-entity retrieval.

queries.query: Did we end up using microservices?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: No — the microservice split was prototyped but reverted due to bugs. The team returned to the monolith (mem 48).
queries.rationale: NEGATION: Memory[48] describes the revert to monolith. The correct answer is NO — microservices were tried and abandoned. Tests whether the system can answer 'did we?' questions with negative answers.

queries.query: What was the outcome of the Excalidraw evaluation?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Excalidraw was proposed by Maria but deferred to post-GA because its sync protocol is incompatible with the CRDT engine (mem 64). Separately, Tomasz evaluated tldraw as an alternative that might work with the CRDT engine — that evaluation is ongoing (mem 70). These are two different diagram initiatives.
queries.rationale: Tests distinction between Excalidraw (deferred) and tldraw (under evaluation). Memory[82] (version history) should NOT surface despite containing 'visual diff' which is tangentially related to diagrams.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests disambiguation of two similar but distinct features.

queries.query: She found that the e2e tests were running against mocks instead of real servers. Who discovered this?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Sarah Kim discovered that Playwright e2e tests were running against a mock WebSocket server (mem 69).
queries.rationale: WHO query with pronoun hint 'she'. Three women on the team (Maria, Sarah, Lena). Memory[69] attributes this to Sarah. Tests person resolution when pronoun narrows but doesn't determine the answer.

queries.query: What happened in the most recent production incident?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Meilisearch index rebuild OOM on a workspace with 2,400 documents (mem 83). NOT the MongoDB failover (mem 75) which was the first incident.
queries.rationale: 'Most recent' = session 6, latest incident. Memory[75] is the first incident, Memory[83] is the second. A recency-blind system might return the higher-importance first incident.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests temporal recency correctly.

queries.query: Who led the post-mortem for the first production incident?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Tomasz led the incident post-mortem (mem 76).
queries.rationale: WHO query. Memory[76] explicitly says 'Incident post-mortem led by Tomasz'. Memory[83] (second incident) should not appear.

queries.query: When did Lena join the team?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Lena joined in session 5 (2 weeks ago). Memory[56] discusses hiring but is from session 4, before she joined.
queries.rationale: Memory[60] describes Lena joining. Memory[56] (hiring discussion) is about planning to hire, not the actual joining.

queries.query: What is our current document load time?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: 220ms p50, 450ms p99 with all beta features (mem 81). NOT the alpha numbers of 180ms/320ms (mem 53) which are outdated.
queries.rationale: CONTRADICTION: Memory[53] (alpha benchmarks) and Memory[81] (current benchmarks) give different numbers. Memory[81] explicitly says the alpha numbers are 'no longer accurate'. Tests whether system returns the CURRENT state, not the earlier one.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests contradiction handling with temporal precedence.

queries.query: What decisions did the team make about monitoring?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Observability stack: OpenTelemetry + Prometheus + Grafana + PagerDuty (mem 22). Later discovered monitoring gaps: no MongoDB replica set lag alerts, no CRDT throughput dashboard (mem 57).
queries.rationale: Two monitoring-related decisions/observations from different sessions.

queries.query: Who was responsible for the offline sync implementation?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Lena Okafor implemented the offline sync queue (mem 62). Originally assigned to Raj (mem 54 says 'complete offline sync (Raj, 3 weeks)') but Lena took it over when she joined (mem 60). She also fixed a state machine bug in it (mem 71).
queries.rationale: IMPLICATION CHAIN: Memory[54] assigns offline sync to Raj, Memory[60] says Lena's first task is offline sync, Memory[62] confirms Lena completed it. Tests whether the system follows the handoff chain. WHO answer is Lena (the implementer), not Raj (the original assignee).
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests role handoff tracking.

queries.query: What improvements came from having new team members?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Lena: faster offline sync delivery (mem 62), CRDT compaction improvement (mem 68), Python SDK bug fix (mem 89). Marcus: security audit findings (mem 61), SOC2 readiness (mem 66), WAF compromise (mem 72), incident playbook (mem 77). Sarah (existing) found mock e2e issue (mem 69). Final retro acknowledges new members brought valuable perspectives (mem 90).
queries.rationale: Requires connecting new members (Lena, Marcus) to their specific contributions across sessions 5-6.

queries.query: He said she should take over the CRDT module. Who is 'he' and who is 'she'?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: 'He' is Raj Patel, 'she' is Lena Okafor. Raj asked Lena to take over the CRDT compaction module based on her offline sync success (mem 91).
queries.rationale: Direct pronoun resolution query. Memory[91] has person_mentions ['raj', 'lena'] as ground truth. The memory itself uses only pronouns, requiring context from other memories or the person_mentions field to resolve.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tests explicit pronoun resolution.

queries.query: What is the difference between the database migration strategy and the database switch?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Database migration (mem 16): schema changes within PostgreSQL using sqlx-migrate. Database switch (mem 18): changing from PostgreSQL to MongoDB for document storage. Memory[93] explicitly disambiguates these two concepts.
queries.rationale: Directly tests the semantic near-miss between 'migration' (schema management) and 'switch' (technology change). Memory[93] is the disambiguation memory.

queries.query: Did we implement real-time collaboration using WebSockets?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Yes — WebSocket gateway for real-time sync is part of the core architecture (mem 6), with detailed scaling design (mem 17).
queries.rationale: Yes/no question with clear positive answer. Tests simple retrieval.

queries.query: Have we completed the SOC2 audit?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: No — the SOC2 Type II audit has begun but is not complete. Marcus has 2 weeks of remaining work (mem 84). Readiness assessment showed 14/22 controls pass (mem 66).
queries.rationale: Current-state temporal query. The audit is in progress, not complete.

queries.query: What features did customers request during beta?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Top requests: version history with visual diff, commenting on text selections, GitHub integration. Top complaints: sync lag on large docs, no mobile app, confusing permission UI.
queries.rationale: Memory[79] contains the customer feedback summary. Memory[64] (Excalidraw) is an internal proposal, not a customer request.

queries.query: Who wrote the incident response playbook?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Marcus Webb wrote the incident response playbook (mem 77).
queries.rationale: WHO query. Memory[77] explicitly says 'He wrote the first incident response playbook' referring to Marcus.

queries.query: What did Diana Chen contribute to the project?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Diana: executive sponsor (mem 0), approved revised timeline (mem 49), approved hiring (mem 56), approved open-source with legal review (mem 86), tried to pull GA forward but accepted pushback (mem 88).
queries.rationale: Person-focused retrieval across all sessions. Tests ability to gather all memories mentioning Diana by role/contribution.

queries.query: What is the notification service built with?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Email via SES, in-app via WebSocket push, frontend notification center by Maria (mem 65).
queries.rationale: Single-hop fact retrieval about notification service implementation.

queries.query: When was the notification service performance a concern?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: It was NOT a concern — performance testing showed negligible latency impact, less than 1ms (mem 94). This contradicts the earlier microservice latency worry because notifications use fire-and-forget NATS publish.
queries.rationale: NEGATION: The answer is that notification performance was NOT a concern. Memory[94] explicitly says 'negligible latency'. Tests understanding of negation in the answer space.

queries.query: Who proposed and who opposed the WAF implementation?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Marcus proposed WAF, Raj opposed it due to latency concerns. They compromised: WAF on REST API only, not WebSocket (mem 72).
queries.rationale: Dual WHO query: proposer AND opposer. Memory[72] names both Marcus and Raj with their positions.

queries.query: What happened with the test coverage target?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Target was 85% (mem 54). Frontend coverage target split: 85% for business logic, 60% for UI components after Maria-Sarah disagreement (mem 92). Actual result: 82% overall, short of target (mem 74).
queries.rationale: CONTRADICTION chain: target set at 85% (mem 54), then frontend target modified (mem 92), then actual result falls short (mem 74). Three-memory synthesis.

queries.query: Why didn't we meet the original GA timeline?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Original GA: month 6 (mem 5). First delay: MongoDB switch pushed alpha back 2 weeks (mem 24). Second delay: combined MongoDB + microservice failure pushed alpha to month 3, GA to month 7 (mem 49).
queries.rationale: IMPLICATION CHAIN: Original timeline (mem 5) -> first delay cause (mem 24) -> cumulative delay (mem 49). Full chain required to explain the gap.

queries.query: What happened between Raj and Lena regarding the compaction strategy?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Raj preferred time-based compaction, Lena argued for size-based. Benchmarks showed trade-offs. Resolution: hybrid approach — size-based for docs over 1MB, time-based for smaller (mem 68).
queries.rationale: Interpersonal technical disagreement with resolution.

queries.query: What features were explicitly deferred or rejected?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Deferred/rejected: GraphQL API (mem 63, security concerns), Excalidraw whiteboard (mem 64, sync incompatibility), AI/LLM features (mem 73, content integrity risk), CockroachDB switch (mem 78, avoiding another database migration).
queries.rationale: NEGATION aggregation: collects all explicitly-rejected or deferred items across the corpus.

queries.query: How many production incidents have we had and what caused them?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Two incidents: (1) MongoDB failover caused 47-second write outage, 23 users lost edits (mem 75). (2) Meilisearch OOM from large workspace index rebuild (mem 83). Memory[48] is a staging bug, not a production incident.
queries.rationale: Counting query requiring exhaustive recall of production incidents.

queries.query: What was decided about the open-source strategy?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: CRDT engine to be open-sourced as nexus-crdt after GA. Raj proposed, Diana approved with legal review requirement (mem 86).
queries.rationale: Direct single-hop retrieval.

queries.query: Who was the first person to find a security issue?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Memory[36] (session 3) describes the first security bug (refresh token invalidation) but does NOT attribute it to a specific person. Memory[61] (session 5, Marcus) describes security findings but is later. The first security issue has no person attribution.
queries.rationale: TEMPORAL + WHO ambiguity: the first security issue (mem 36, session 3) predates Marcus joining. It has no person_mentions. A system that returns Marcus (mem 61) is wrong on temporal ordering. Tests whether system correctly identifies the FIRST occurrence even when person attribution is absent.
queries.reviewer_notes: Tricky: earliest security finding has no person attribution.

queries.query: What project management tools do we use?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Linear for backlog management, two-week sprints, GitHub Actions for CI (mem 14).
queries.rationale: Single-hop fact retrieval about project tools.

queries.query: How did the team handle disagreements?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: Multiple patterns: RFC process for formal decisions (mem 52), benchmark-driven resolution (mem 68 compaction debate), compromise approaches (mem 72 WAF, mem 85 CI), team voting (mem 88 GA date).
queries.rationale: Open-domain synthesis of disagreement resolution patterns across the project.

queries.query: Is the notification service causing performance problems?
queries.expected_answer_sketch: No — less than 1ms latency impact, fire-and-forget NATS publish (mem 94).
queries.rationale: Negation: the answer is explicitly no. Memory[94] provides evidence of absence of performance problems.

scoring.note: Weights: 30% MRR + 20% R@5 + 15% R@10 + 20% P@5 + 15% absence compliance. SCORE-1 fix applied.
